title: Cat Abdominal Heaving: When to Seek Emergency Veterinary Care
meta_desc: Is your cat's belly moving heavily while breathing? Abdominal heaving is a high-urgency emergency. Learn why and how to help your pet immediately.
content_html:
TL;DR: Abdominal heaving in cats is a life-threatening medical emergency where a cat uses their stomach muscles to breathe. If you notice forceful belly movements, seek immediate veterinary care to prevent respiratory arrest.

What is abdominal heaving in cats and what does it look like?

Abdominal heaving, also known as labored breathing or dyspnea, occurs when a cat must use their stomach muscles to assist in moving air in and out of the lungs. Unlike normal, quiet breathing where the chest moves slightly, heaving is visible, forceful, and indicates that the cat is working much harder than normal to oxygenate their body.

Is abdominal heaving in cats considered a medical emergency?

  • Abdominal heaving is classified as a high-urgency medical emergency.
  • Because cats are stoic and hide illness, visible belly movement with every breath indicates the respiratory system is failing to keep up.
  • This condition requires immediate veterinary intervention to prevent respiratory arrest.

Why should I take a video of my cat’s breathing for the veterinarian?

  • A short 10-to-15 second video allows the triage team to see the exact effort, rate, and rhythm of the breathing.
  • Videos provide an objective look at the symptoms, which is more helpful than a subjective description of "heavy breathing."
  • Seeing the video allows the emergency clinic to prepare oxygen therapy or life-saving equipment before you even arrive.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

In cats, abdominal discomfort accompanied by persistent vomiting, depression, or fever warrants immediate veterinary reevaluation. Signs of hypovolemic shock or abdominal pain also necessitate emergency care, as these can indicate intestinal incarceration or gastrointestinal perforation with secondary bacterial peritonitis. Physical examination should include a thorough inspection of the oral cavity for linear foreign bodies anchored to the base of the tongue, which should be cut immediately if found.

Chapter: Gastroenterology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 390)

faqs.question: Can I wait until morning to see my regular veterinarian?
faqs.answer: No. Respiratory distress can progress to a life-threatening crisis in minutes. You should go to the nearest 24-hour emergency animal hospital immediately.

faqs.question: What are other signs of respiratory distress in cats?
faqs.answer: In addition to abdominal heaving, watch for open-mouth breathing (panting), blue or purple-tinged gums, or a 'hunched' posture with the neck extended forward.

faqs.question: What should I do while transporting my cat to the vet?
faqs.answer: Keep the car cool and quiet. Avoid stressing the cat further by talking loudly or playing music. If possible, keep them in a carrier to prevent injury if they panic due to lack of air.
